text: In the Gita's sixth chapter, Krishna describes the disciplined mind using the image of a lamp in a windless place — its flame unwavering, undistracted. This metaphor is precise. A lamp in wind does not go out; it merely flickers, consuming more fuel to produce the same light. The undisciplined mind similarly wastes enormous energy on micro-corrections: suppressing intrusive thoughts, recovering attention, re-engaging with interrupted work. Neurologically, each act of recovery costs prefrontal resources. Krishna's windless lamp suggests an environment and an inner disposition so settled that recovery never becomes necessary — because drift never begins.
question: In the passage's interpretation of Krishna's lamp metaphor, what does the flickering flame represent?

options.text: The spiritual insight that arises during moments of deep meditation
The mind extinguished by excessive distraction and loss of discipline
A mind wasting energy on micro-corrections rather than going out entirely
The transition from karma yoga to jnana yoga in the Gita's teaching
